restore-without-rewriting-history.label: Recover from Everyday Mistakes
restore-without-rewriting-history.summary: Create supplied temporary mistakes and recover them without rewriting history.

sketch2.title: A Staged Mistake May Need Two Actions

try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.title: Restore an Unstaged README Mistake
try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.prompt: Open `community-project/README.md`. Find `Shared schedules and contact notes.` and replace it with `Accidental rewrite.` Save the file and inspect the unstaged diff. Then restore the committed README and confirm with short status that no tracked change remains.
try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.hint: Review the mistake first, then restore only the working-tree copy.

help.concept: git restore PATH discards an unstaged working-tree change to that path.
help.hint_1: Replace only the supplied README sentence.
help.hint_2: The existing commits must remain unchanged.

try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.starterCode:
# Community Project

Shared schedules and contact notes.
try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.solutionCode:
git diff -- README.md
git restore README.md
git status --short
try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.instructions: Open `community-project/README.md`. Find `Shared schedules and contact notes.` and replace it with `Accidental rewrite.` Save the file and inspect the unstaged diff. Then restore the committed README and confirm with short status that no tracked change remains.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf -- community-project
mkdir -p -- community-project
git -C community-project init -q -b main
git -C community-project config --local user.name 'ZoeSkoul Learner'
git -C community-project config --local user.email learner@zoeskoul.local
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s '# Community Project

Shared schedules and contact notes.
' > community-project/README.md
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community schedule

Saturday cleanup: 10:00 AM
' > community-project/schedule.txt
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community contacts

Help desk: 555-0100
' > community-project/contacts.txt
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Old planning notes

Spring flyer draft.
' > community-project/old-notes.txt
mkdir -p -- community-project/generated
printf %s 'Generated summary

Created automatically from project notes.
' > community-project/generated/summary.txt
git -C community-project add -A
GIT_AUTHOR_DATE=2026-01-01T12:00:00Z GIT_COMMITTER_DATE=2026-01-01T12:00:00Z git -C community-project commit -q -m 'Start community project'
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community schedule

Saturday cleanup: 9:30 AM
' > community-project/schedule.txt
git -C community-project add -A
GIT_AUTHOR_DATE=2026-01-02T12:00:00Z GIT_COMMITTER_DATE=2026-01-02T12:00:00Z git -C community-project commit -q -m 'Update community schedule'
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community contacts

Help desk: 555-0130
' > community-project/contacts.txt
git -C community-project add -A
GIT_AUTHOR_DATE=2026-01-03T12:00:00Z GIT_COMMITTER_DATE=2026-01-03T12:00:00Z git -C community-project commit -q -m 'Update community contacts'
rm -f -- community-project/schedule.txt
rm -f -- community-project/old-notes.txt
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community schedule

Saturday cleanup: 9:30 AM
' > community-project/events.txt
git -C community-project add -A
GIT_AUTHOR_DATE=2026-01-04T12:00:00Z GIT_COMMITTER_DATE=2026-01-04T12:00:00Z git -C community-project commit -q -m 'Organize community project files'
rm -f -- community-project/generated/summary.txt
rmdir -- community-project/generated 2>/dev/null || true
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'generated/
*.log
' > community-project/.gitignore
git -C community-project add -A
GIT_AUTHOR_DATE=2026-01-05T12:00:00Z GIT_COMMITTER_DATE=2026-01-05T12:00:00Z git -C community-project commit -q -m 'Ignore generated output'
mkdir -p -- community-project/generated
printf %s 'Generated summary

Created automatically from project notes.
' > community-project/generated/summary.txt
mkdir -p -- community-project/generated
printf %s '' > community-project/generated/preview.txt
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s '' > community-project/debug.log

0.message: That action can discard or rewrite history. Use the safe, layer-specific workflow taught in this course.
Did you inspect the unstaged README mistake?

1.message: Did you restore the committed README?

2.message: Did you verify that the tracked state is clean?

try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.title: Stage and Unstage a Contact Mistake
try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.prompt: Open `community-project/contacts.txt`. Replace the entire file with exactly these three lines: `Community contacts`, a blank line, and `Help desk: 000-0000`. Save it, stage the file, then remove it from the staging area without changing the working copy. Do not restore the file yet.
try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.hint: This step corrects only the staging-area mistake; the bad text should remain unstaged.

help.concept: git restore --staged removes a path from the proposed snapshot but preserves its working-tree content.
help.hint_1: Replace the complete file with the one supplied line.
help.hint_2: contacts.txt should remain modified after unstaging.

try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.solutionCode:
git add contacts.txt
git restore --staged contacts.txt
try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.instructions: Open `community-project/contacts.txt`. Replace the entire file with exactly these three lines: `Community contacts`, a blank line, and `Help desk: 000-0000`. Save it, stage the file, then remove it from the staging area without changing the working copy. Do not restore the file yet.

1.message: Did you unstage it while preserving the working copy?

try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.title: Restore the Remaining Contact Mistake
try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.prompt: The incorrect help-desk number remains unstaged in `contacts.txt`. Restore the committed file, then confirm that short status has no unfinished tracked changes.
try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.hint: Restore the working-tree layer after the staging area is already clean.

help.concept: After unstaging preserves the edited file, a separate working-tree restore removes the unwanted content.
help.hint_2: The final short status should be empty.

try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.instructions: The incorrect help-desk number remains unstaged in `contacts.txt`. Restore the committed file, then confirm that short status has no unfinished tracked changes.

0.message: That action can discard or rewrite history. Use the safe, layer-specific workflow taught in this course.
Did you restore the committed contact file?

1.message: Did you verify that no unfinished tracked change remains?

single-choice-1.title: Purpose of git restore
single-choice-1.prompt: What is the primary purpose of `git restore path` in this course?
single-choice-1.hint: Focus on what the command or concept observes or changes at this point in the workflow.

help.concept: Correct working-tree and staging mistakes at the right layer while leaving existing commits untouched.
help.hint_1: Remove options that act on a different repository layer than the one named in the question.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that remains accurate even when other unrelated files exist.

options.a: To discard an unwanted working-tree edit

single-choice-2.title: Effect of git restore --staged
single-choice-2.prompt: What happens when you use `git restore --staged` on a file?
single-choice-2.hint: Focus on what the command or concept observes or changes at this point in the workflow.

help.concept: Correct working-tree and staging mistakes at the right layer while leaving existing commits untouched.
help.hint_1: Remove options that act on a different repository layer than the one named in the question.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that remains accurate even when other unrelated files exist.

options.a: The file is removed from the staging area but changes are kept
options.b: The file is deleted from the repository
options.c: The file is committed to the repository
options.d: The file is reverted to the last committed state

multi-choice-1.title: Choosing the Correct Restore Command
multi-choice-1.prompt: Which commands can discard an unwanted working-tree edit and unstage a file?
multi-choice-1.hint: Judge each option independently; choose only the statements that stay true in this exact repository state.

help.concept: Correct working-tree and staging mistakes at the right layer while leaving existing commits untouched.
help.hint_1: A partly true option is still wrong if it adds an effect Git does not guarantee.
help.hint_2: Use the layer named in the prompt—working tree, staging area, or history—to test each choice.

drag-reorder-1.title: Discard a Change That Is Already Staged
drag-reorder-1.prompt: Arrange the safe correction steps for a file whose accidental rewrite is both staged and present in the working tree.
drag-reorder-1.hint: Start with the state that must exist before the next action can make sense.

help.concept: Correct working-tree and staging mistakes at the right layer while leaving existing commits untouched.
help.hint_1: Track how the project moves from working files to a proposed snapshot and then to history.
help.hint_2: Read the ordered steps as one continuous repository story.

tokens.t1: Remove the accidental version from the staging area
tokens.t2: Restore the working file from the recorded version

restore-sketch-0.title: Restore the Layer You Intend
restore-sketch-0.bodyMarkdown: `git restore path` replaces an unstaged working-tree edit with the committed version. It does not rewrite earlier commits.

restore-sketch-1.title: Unstage Without Losing the File
restore-sketch-1.bodyMarkdown: `git restore --staged path` removes a file from the proposed snapshot while preserving its current working-tree text.

restore-sketch-2.title: A Staged Mistake May Need Two Actions
restore-sketch-2.bodyMarkdown: When the same unwanted edit is staged and still present in the file, unstage it first and restore the working copy second. The two-step recovery makes each layer explicit.
